metadata.title: How to Find a Job When the System Is Broken: The OPT Scraper Story
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.

tags: How to Find a Job When the System Is Bro
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The first obstacle isn't finding jobs, it's finding the findable ones hidden behind ATS systems. Greenhouse and Lever together cover the companies that actually matter for these searches. A scraper against those systems surfaces roles like Duolingo's 83 open positions directly. Integrating scrapers into a verification pipeline against SEC Form D data reveals a measurable hit rate.

artifactLines: The first obstacle isn't finding jobs, it's finding the findable ones hidden behind ATS systems.
Greenhouse and Lever together cover the companies that actually matter for these searches.
A scraper against those systems surfaces roles like Duolingo's 83 open positions directly.
Integrating scrapers into a verification pipeline against SEC Form D data reveals a measurable hit rate.

beats.narration_text: The real bottleneck isn't finding jobs, it's finding the findable ones behind the ATS layer. I want to build the scraper you described for myself. Walk me through: (1) how to hit Greenhouse and Lever endpoints to pull openings from a company like Duolingo's 83 roles, (2) how to feed those results into a verification pipeline cross-referenced against an SEC Form D list, and (3) how I'd measure the hit rate to know the scraper is actually working.

output: You open the Greenhouse API at 3 AM
This is what it looks like when bureaucracy meets code
Eighty days before the deadline becomes literal: stay or go home
